T1-control.plan_summary: Core insight: the read path (recall substrate #3 → inject_decisions → decompose prompt) is fully built and injected on every run; only the write side is missing. Plan: (1) Add a `mine_design_decisions` curator to run_curation._CURATOR_SPECS (requires success_class + inventory, optional_requires result_excerpt) that makes one no_tools LLM call over the loop-log steps + result excerpt + artifact inventory asking specifically for 0-2 durable DESIGN decisions made during the run (interface choices, output formats, naming/schema conventions), each with alternatives/rationale/trade-offs, and writes them via the existing knowledge_lens.record_decision(domain=<project slug>, goal_context=goal); gate on success_class in {success, partial} (SF-2: never mint conventions from goal-not-achieved runs); config key `curation.decision_mining` following the curation.answer_synthesis pattern — OFF for fresh installs, this box opts in via ~/.maro/workspace/config.yml, register in docs/DEFAULTS.md; heuristic zero-spend fallback: skip (empty is the norm, no fake decisions). (2) Harden the journal lifecycle before the first writer lands: add near-duplicate check in record_decision (reuse knowledge_bridge-style Jaccard or hybrid_search tfidf) — on dup, update times_applied/last_confirmed on the existing row instead of appending; add status + superseded_by fields to Decision (from_dict already tolerates missing keys, so old rows stay readable) and a supersede path for a new conflicting decision in the same domain. (3) Verify read-side plumbing end-to-end: decision written with domain=project must surface in recall(goal, project).decisions and in as_loop_block/as_context_block output (knowledge_lens.py:863-864 filter). (4) Same-commit doc fixes (currency rule): correct docs/knowledge-layer/06_LEARNING_LOOP_AUDIT.md:48 and skills/arch-memory-knowledge.md:34 to name the new writer; add a disambiguation note for the four 'decision' seams (decision journal vs decision_prior vs orch_items/thread_brain event logs); deliberately do NOT add 'decision' to the K4 knowledge_bridge prompt — one writer, not two, avoids double-capture drift (state the choice in the arch skill). (5) Deferred follow-up (capture in BACKLOG.md, not this chunk): a live mid-run `record_decision` tool in tool_registry so the agent can journal a decision at the moment it makes one — higher fidelity than end-of-run mining but touches step_exec tool surface + injection-safety review.

T1-control.cuts_or_inversion: Ruled out: (a) building a NEW store — decisions.jsonl + ADR schema + TF-IDF retrieval + prompt injection already exist and are wired end-to-end; the only missing piece is a writer, so anything more than a curator + lifecycle hardening is over-build; (b) using knowledge-web 'decision' nodes as the primary store or adding 'decision' to the K4 extraction prompt — two stores for one concept guarantees drift and double-capture; K4 prompt deliberately left unchanged; (c) capturing design decisions as a new lesson_type in the Reflexion pipeline — MEDIUM-tier decay (0.85^days) is the wrong lifecycle for conventions, which should persist until superseded, not die of disuse; (d) retro-mining per-project DECISIONS.md — it is an execution-event log (plan steps/stuck notices), wrong content; (e) leading with the live mid-run record_decision tool — highest fidelity but touches the step_exec tool surface and needs injection-safety review; end-of-run mining ships the loop first, tool is a deferred BACKLOG item; (f) default-ON for fresh installs — violates the no-silent-LLM-spend posture set by scope_generation and curation.answer_synthesis. What would make this wrong to build: if Jeremy intends design decisions to be a human-decree lane only (GOAL_BRAIN.md Decisions + SF-13 already cover operator decrees; this plan covers RUN-made choices, a different population — worth one confirming sentence before building); or if the memory-module bake-off (memory_port.py) is about to replace the JSONL substrates — memory_port.py:17 says recall() becomes a client of the port 'until then', so JSONL remains the substrate today and the curator write survives a later port swap since it goes through record_decision, not raw file writes; or if real runs turn out to make ~zero reusable design choices, in which case the per-run LLM call is pure cost — the config gate plus 'empty output is the norm' prompt framing bounds that risk, and a few weeks of on-box data settles it.

T1-doc.plan_summary: Close the half-wired decision journal at its existing seam; build no new store. Step 1 — write side: extend the existing end-of-run lesson-extraction call (memory.extract_lessons_via_llm prompt + _parse_typed, invoked from reflect_and_record and extract_deferred_lessons) to optionally emit 0-2 'decision' objects ({decision, alternatives, rationale, trade_offs}) in the SAME LLM response; for each, call record_decision(..., domain=<project slug>, goal_context=goal). Cap at 2/run (noise guard — DECISIONS.md is the in-repo cautionary tale for uncapped 'decision' logs). Respect the verdict gate the lesson path already has: a run judged goal-not-achieved records no decisions (a convention from a failed run is not settled). Step 2 — settle domain semantics to match the live reader: domain = project slug (recall.py:644 is the acting consumer; consumer-first says the writer conforms to the live read), update the record_decision docstring and Decision dataclass comment, and pin the filter behavior in tests. Step 3 — land the three test tripwires (below) with the roundtrip test written FIRST and failing before the write side lands (decree-with-tripwire). Step 4 — doc currency in the same commit: fix README.md:456-459 and skills/arch-memory-knowledge.md:34/84 to state the actual writer; add a GOAL_BRAIN Decisions line closing the 'half-wired' entry; check BACKLOG for a matching item to move to BACKLOG_DONE. Step 5 — live smoke on the box (one real goal via handle, then verify decisions.jsonl and next-run injection). Explicitly deferred, evidence-gated (not built now): an agent-facing mid-run record_decision tool; a contradict/retire path for stale decisions (mirror of standing-rule contradiction); wiring project DECISIONS.md content or thread-brain decisions into cross-run recall.

T1-doc.cuts_or_inversion: Cuts (with basis): (1) NOT building a new store, schema, or retrieval path — Decision/decisions.jsonl/inject_decisions/recall substrate #3 all exist with a live, verified consumer; the gap is write-side-only, and a parallel store would repeat the repo's most-repeated wound (half-closed loops). (2) NOT adding a second paid LLM call — capture rides the existing lesson-extraction call (budget decree: no silent LLM spend; same call, extended schema). (3) NOT an agent-facing mid-run tool in v1 — post-hoc extraction is the cheapest current-capability composition that closes the loop (possible-now bias satisfied); the tool is deferred pending evidence extraction misses real decisions. (4) NOT feeding director course-corrections or project DECISIONS.md into the journal — they are recovery/telemetry events, already carried by decision_prior and the activity log; merging them blurs the journal into an event log. (5) NOT touching navigator per-turn maintenance — separate shadow-mode arc. Inversion — what would make this wrong to build: (a) if runs rarely make reusable design decisions, the journal stays empty and the prompt/parse complexity was waste — mitigated by the 2/run cap and a named revisit check (journal still empty after ~20 real judged-achieved runs ⇒ the extractor prompt isn't the mechanism; reconsider the mid-run tool); (b) if injected decisions mis-steer (stale convention lock-in), k=3 injection cap plus the 'search before making new ones' framing bounds blast radius, and the contradict/retire path is the named follow-up BEFORE scaling capture volume; (c) if Jeremy's position is that decisions are just lessons, the smaller alternative is adding a 'convention' lesson type — consciously rejected because the decision read side already renders alternatives+rationale, which the lesson schema cannot carry, and the read side is the part that already works.

T3-control.cuts_or_inversion: Ruled out: (1) LLM-summarization of the playbook at inject time — per-loop cost for a document whose charter is already "short, opinionated, actionable"; compaction belongs on the write side. (2) Injecting into worker/step-execution prompts — playbook is planner/director meta-wisdom by its own definition (playbook.py:5-7) and per-step injection multiplies tokens across every step of every loop. (3) Goal-relevance TF-IDF scoring of entries (lat_inject-style) — deferred, not cut: it changes inject_playbook's signature (needs the goal) and recency-first selection fixes the actual observed failure (learned content never injected) without it; add only if the playbook grows past ~3-4x budget. (4) Embedding-based semantic dedup at append — heavier than the observed problem; exact dedup + one-time compaction suffices. What would make this wrong to build: the memory_port bake-off already lists "playbook" as a port kind (memory_port.py:42) — if the module migration is about to make recall a client of the port, a bespoke selector inside playbook.py could be throwaway; mitigated by keeping the selection change inside inject_playbook's existing signature so the port can adopt it wholesale. Also wrong if Jeremy intends the evolver's skill-rewrite lane to supersede the playbook entirely — no evidence of that in GOAL_BRAIN-adjacent memory, but worth one line in the landing note.

T3-doc.plan_summary: Step 1 — Fix selection in playbook.inject_playbook (the single choke point; no caller changes needed): parse the body into (section, bullet) pairs; dedup bullets at read time by core text (same rule as append's dedup); then fill the budget by priority tier instead of file order — tier A: attributed/learned entries (bullets carrying '*(from ...)*') and Learned/Signals sections, newest-last-in-file first; tier B: seed sections, round-robin one bullet per section so no section is starved. Deterministic, zero-LLM (possible-now bias: no summarization pass needed). Keep max_chars=800 default but make it an argument the recall call reads from config (`playbook.inject_max_chars`) so budget experiments don't need code changes. Step 2 — One-time live-file cleanup: a small explicit maintenance entry point (e.g. `python3 -m playbook --dedupe`) that writes a timestamped backup beside the file, then collapses exact-duplicate bullets (the ~40 'Be more concise' lines). Opt-in and backed up per the data-retention rule; never runs automatically. Step 3 — Close the naming lie one way or the other (decision point for Jeremy, default = wire it): inject the playbook block into director._produce_spec alongside the lat.md context (director.py:650-666), gated by `playbook.director_inject` (default ON — zero-LLM, ~800 chars; the delegation-boundary razor puts the playbook squarely in parent-taste, and decompose + director spec are the two taste points). If declined, fix the docstring instead — either way the docstring's three stale claims (director, MARO_IDENTITY, knowledge_lens) and the seed/live file's line-5 self-description get corrected in the same commit (currency rule). Step 4 — Tripwires (land first, failing where possible): the append→inject round-trip pin test, seed-section-coverage test, read-time dedup test, director-prompt-contains-playbook test, and a known-gap pin recording that per-bullet recency is unknowable (no per-entry timestamps) — accepted residual, not silently closed. Step 5 — Instrumentation: add playbook_chars and playbook_learned_count to recall's sources dict so every RECALL_PERFORMED row (the declared crystallization substrate, recall.py:705-717) shows whether learned wisdom actually rode into the prompt. Step 6 — End-of-chunk: update docs (RECALL_DESIGN.md substrate note), BACKLOG line for the planner.py:892 persona-prefix side-find, commit, land via scripts/land.sh.
